text: This is a young male who's a power lifter, was dead lifting over four hundred pounds and felt a popping sensation in his right groin. Presented to me in the office in the elective outpatient setting. You can see here he's very thin, very muscular guy, not much preperitoneal fat. Working laterally towards bigrosis space, preperitoneal plane is completely free of any kind of scarring, but all of a sudden there's no signs of fat. So I'm able to take it down pretty easily.
The surgeon works medially toward the supravesicular space, revealing the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum converging at the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon scores the thickened hernia sac as it enters the deep internal ring on the medial side, using cautery to push fat away laterally for better visualization. The vas deferens is not yet visible but is expected to come into view shortly. The surgeon performs medial dissection below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, eventually revealing the vas deferens. The hernia sac appears edematous and relatively small but symptomatic.

The surgeon uses an oblique angle to push down the spermatic vessels, revealing the right iliac vein close to the tissue. The proximity is necessary to ensure the tissue is properly positioned, preventing mesh buckling at this spot during the dissection.

The iliac vein is visible on the right side of the surgical field. The surgeon ensures the pocket is well tailored, with high lateral fixation to prevent mesh migration. The surgeon checks the other side of the mesh to confirm no vascular structures are present.
The surgeon performs a straightforward flap closure, ensuring the surgical site is properly sealed.
